aliases: Potato and Cauliflower Curry with Butter Naan

english: Potato and Cauliflower Curry with Butter Naan

food_subgroup: Indian vegetarian curry with flatbread (potato-cauliflower sabzi served with butter naan)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan as a combined meal. FOODON_03311069 (potato and cauliflower dish) is used as the closest parent for the curry component. The naan component is not separately coded here.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan as a combined dish. A0CKP (Potato dishes) is used as the closest parent for the dominant curry component. The butter naan component is not separately represented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan as a combined dish. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this Indian composite meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan consists of approximately 250 g of potato-cauliflower curry accompanied by one butter naan of approximately 130 g, totalling around 380 g per plate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Aloo Gobi (potato and cauliflower curry) and Butter Naan composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition), Hyderabad, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for Aloo Gobi sabzi and plain/butter naan components. A weighted composite per-100 g value was calculated assuming a 66:34 ratio of curry to naan by weight (250 g curry : 130 g naan per serving). Butter naan values are adapted from ICMR-NIN naan/chapati entries with added butter (approximately 5 g butter per naan). This is an adapted estimate; no single ICMR-NIN entry covers this exact combined dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Potato-based curry dishes and wheat flatbread (naan) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan as a combined dish. GI is estimated as a weighted composite: boiled potato GI ~78 (University of Sydney GI Database), cauliflower GI ~15 (low-GI vegetable, University of Sydney GI Database), and white wheat naan GI ~71 (closest analogous flatbread from University of Sydney GI Database, similar to white bread/chapati). Weighted composite GI calculated using carbohydrate contribution of each component per serving: potato ~35 g CHO, cauliflower ~5 g CHO, naan ~33 g CHO. Weighted GI ≈ (35×78 + 5×15 + 33×71) / 73 ≈ 62. GL per serving = GI/100 × available CHO per serving (g) = 62/100 × 39.5 g ≈ 24.5. Classified as medium GI (56–69 range).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1178 mg per serving) from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) driven by potato and wheat naan components; moderate carbohydrate load (~96.5 g CHO per full serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (half plate) and pair with a low-GI side such as raita or salad to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the butter naan component contributes significantly to the glycemic load and may be substituted with a smaller portion of whole-wheat roti.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~2.1 g/100 g; ~8 g per serving) and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit butter naan intake and request the dish prepared with minimal added butter or ghee, as the saturated fat and sodium content of a full serving may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan is a classic North Indian vegetarian meal consisting of two components: Aloo Gobi sabzi (a dry or semi-dry curry of potatoes and cauliflower cooked with onion, tomato, cumin, turmeric, coriander, ginger, garlic, and garam masala) served alongside one or more butter naan (a soft leavened wheat flatbread baked in a tandoor oven and finished with a generous spread of butter). The dish originates from Punjabi cuisine and is widely popular across the Indian subcontinent and in Indian restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it is a staple offering at Indian vegetarian restaurants. The curry has a warm, earthy, mildly spiced flavour with a soft-tender texture from the cooked potato and a slightly firm bite from the cauliflower florets. The butter naan is pillowy, slightly charred on the surface, and rich from the butter coating. Nutritionally, the combined meal is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (~25 g/100 g) from both the potato and wheat flour, moderate fat (~5.8 g/100 g) primarily from butter and cooking oil, and modest protein (~4.2 g/100 g) from wheat and legume traces. The dish provides dietary fibre (~2.6 g/100 g) from cauliflower and potato skins, vitamin C from cauliflower, and iron and B vitamins from the wheat naan. Clinically, the medium glycemic index (~62) and moderate sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten (wheat) and dairy (butter), making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or lactose intolerance.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Aloo Gobi with Naan?
Is Aloo Gobi good for diabetics?
What is butter naan made of?
What are the ingredients in Aloo Gobi?
What is the glycemic index of Aloo Gobi?
Can I eat Aloo Gobi with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry covers Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan as a combined restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component entries (Aloo Gobi sabzi and naan/chapati with butter) using a weighted composite calculation based on a typical restaurant serving ratio. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact combined-dish entry was found in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. The input food name 'Aloo Gobi ❤️ Free Butter Naan' appears to be a GrabFood promotional listing where 'Free Butter Naan' indicates a complimentary naan accompaniment with the Aloo Gobi order. The canonical food name has been normalised to 'Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan' to reflect the actual dish composition. 2. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for the curry and naan components separately; no single approved source entry covers this exact combined dish. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based vegetarian dish with no animal-derived ingredients except butter, which contributes negligible B12; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN plant food entries. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (weighted composite ~62, medium GI) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for boiled potato, cauliflower, and wheat naan/flatbread components; no direct GI measurement for this combined dish was found. 6. The restaurant listed (Jewel Of India, Boat Quay, Singapore) is an Indian vegetarian restaurant; the dish is confirmed vegetarian and halal-suitable. 7. Price SGD 22.00 is preserved from the GrabFood source record for reference only and is not used in nutritional assessment.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalised food_name from promotional GrabFood listing to canonical 'Aloo Gobi with Butter Naan'. Added full multilingual local_names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) to satisfy G6. Computed weighted composite per_100g nutrients from ICMR-NIN component data. Estimated GI as weighted composite from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Added full health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease. Populated ontology with closest_parent codes and gap notes. Wrote LLM description >200 characters. Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency verification. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) and zero values (vitamin_b12_mcg) in notes.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language to flat aliases consistency — all entries confirmed present verbatim. Verified zero-macro justification rule — vitamin_b12_mcg=0.0 documented as confirmed zero for plant-based dish. Verified sentinel value documentation for vitamin_d_mcg=-1 and selenium_mcg=-1. Verified ontology match_type values are exclusively 'exact', 'closest_parent', or 'none'. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass. Decision: PASS.
